subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

reviews.author: Huang, Ying
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle kernel allocations correctly, as it relies on alloc_pages_mpol(), which is intended for user space applications, and suggested using MPOL_LOCAL instead to allocate from local nodes first
reviews.raw_body:
Yes.  The page isn't allocated through alloc_pages_mpol().  For example,
if we want to allocate pages for the kernel instead of user space
applications.  However, IMHO, the equivalent memory policy is
MPOL_LOCAL, that is, allocate from local node firstly, then fallback to
other nodes.  I don't think that alloc_pages_mpol() is so special.

---
Best Regards,
Huang, Ying

reviews.author: JP (Meta) (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that they took the reviewer's statement about default memory policy usage 'literally' and agreed on behavioral equivalence, but emphasized the distinction between allocations with and without a user-defined mempolicy in their v3 patch.
reviews.raw_body:
Sure. My response was based on how you said, "the default memory policy
is used here". I took that literally. I agree on the behavioral
equivalence, but the important point is that no mempolicy is set. In the
v3 patch which was recently sent out, I'm using that aspect to
distinguish between allocations with a user-defined mempolicy and
allocations without one.

2026-03-17.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to track page allocations per memory policy, allowing for more detailed statistics and debugging capabilities. The problem it solves is the lack of visibility into how different memory policies are affecting system performance and resource allocation. The approach taken involves modifying the alloc_pages_mpol function to collect and report allocation statistics on a per-mempolicy basis.
